name: Adam Sandler
blurb: The everyman-meathead voice of comedy rock: stand-up done with an acoustic guitar, a goofy character, and a chorus the room can sing along to by the bridge.

name: Ali Wong
blurb: Feral and candid; demolisher of the polite hour.

name: Andy Griffith
blurb: The pre-Carlin, pre-Cosby pioneer of the long comic monologue — 'What It Was, Was Football' in 1953 essentially invented the modern stand-up record.

name: Bernie Mac
blurb: Kings of Comedy force; fearless storyteller.

name: Bill Burr
blurb: The everyman rant; talks hostile crowds into love.

name: Bill Hicks
blurb: Preacher-philosopher of the counterculture.

name: Bo Burnham
blurb: Musical-comedy auteur of the internet age.

name: Bob Newhart
blurb: Deadpan button-down mind; the one-sided phone call.

name: Brad Garrett
blurb: The voice — six-foot-eight, baritone, a working impressionist who won Star Search at 23 and never stopped being a road-headlining stand-up under the Robert Barone fame.
photo_credit: Marc Levin via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY 2.0)

name: Chris Rock
blurb: Razor social commentary with a closer's timing.

name: Damon Wayans
blurb: The middle Wayans who almost got it both ways — fired from SNL for going too gay, then turned around and built In Living Color into a Black-comedy machine the networks had no answer to.
photo_credit: Brian Solis via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY 2.0)

name: Dave Chappelle
blurb: Street logic, political nerve, generational ease.

name: Denis Leary
blurb: The Boston-Irish rant comic with a cigarette in one hand and a beer in the other, who turned MTV interstitials and one Showtime hour into the angriest stand-up brand of the 1990s.

name: Eddie Izzard
blurb: Stream-of-consciousness surrealist in heels.

name: Eddie Murphy
blurb: Leather-suited supernova of the arena era.

name: George Carlin
blurb: Counterculture philosopher of language and taboo.

name: Hannah Gadsby
blurb: Interrogated the form itself in Nanette.

name: Hasan Minhaj
blurb: Cinematic political storytelling; the new docu-special.

name: Jay Mohr
blurb: The Jersey impressionist's impressionist — Walken, Pacino, Tracy Morgan, Norm — wrapped around two-and-a-half years of SNL trauma turned into the best memoir an SNL cast member has ever written.

name: Jeff Garlin
blurb: The Chicago improv lifer who walks onstage with no set, talks until something is funny, and pulls it off because of taste.

name: Jim Carrey
blurb: The physical-impressionist who hammered Yuk Yuk's and the Comedy Store into a one-man rubber-face engine, then weaponized it for In Living Color and the entire mid-1990s box office.

name: John Mulaney
blurb: Precision-built storytelling with old-soul polish.

name: John Pinette
blurb: The buffet whisperer. Food, accents, and the unmatchable mock-French of someone who has seen the lobster.

name: Larry David
blurb: The Brooklyn-kid-as-conscience: a stand-up career he largely abandoned, then ran back through the writers' room of Seinfeld and the persona of Curb until everyday discomfort became a national language.

name: Larry Miller
blurb: The Five Levels of Drinking guy — an Amherst-trained monologuist whose taxonomy of getting hammered is one of the most-quoted American stand-up bits no comic has ever managed to outdo.

name: Lenny Bruce
blurb: The First Amendment martyr who made stand-up dangerous.

name: Maddy Kelly
blurb: Vancouver-to-LA half-Irish, half-Indian sharpshooter; Tonight Show debut at 25; writes the Canadian sketches that go viral.

name: Mark Normand
blurb: Joke-dense club assassin; punchlines at speed.

name: Matt Rife
blurb: Crowd-work phenomenon of the TikTok era.

name: Mel Brooks
blurb: The 2000 Year Old Man — a five-album improv duet with Carl Reiner that is the foundational pre-modern stand-up record, half a millennium of Jewish-grandfather wisdom answered into Reiner's straight-man microphone.

name: Michael McIntyre
blurb: The bouncing-on-his-heels arena observationalist — the man who turned the contents of every British man's kitchen drawer into a sold-out Wembley bit.

name: Mitch Hedberg
blurb: Surreal one-liner mystic of the absurd non sequitur.

name: Mort Sahl
blurb: Newspaper in hand, the pioneer of political stand-up.

name: Nate Bargatze
blurb: Clean, slow-burn deadpan; the everyman headliner.

name: Nick Kroll
blurb: Character-comedy machine: Kroll Show, the Oh, Hello geezers, and Big Mouth.

name: Norm Crosby
blurb: The Master of Malaprop — a one-trick act so meticulously committed that the trick became one of the great running gags in American comedy.

name: Norm Macdonald
blurb: Anti-comedy assassin; the patron saint of the long setup.

name: Patton Oswalt
blurb: Nerd-poet of pop culture and grief.

name: Richard Pryor
blurb: The most influential stand-up who ever lived.

name: Rip Taylor
blurb: The Crying Comedian who turned a bad set into a sixty-year career — moustache, toupee, confetti by the bucket, and a voice pitched to the back of the room whether the room held twelve people or twelve hundred.

name: Robert Klein
blurb: The observational bridge into the comedy-album era.

name: Robin Williams
blurb: Improvisational tornado; free association at light speed.

name: Rowan Atkinson
blurb: The face is the bit. Before Bean, before Blackadder, he was a solo stand-up — the Schoolmaster, the Devil welcoming you to Hell, the Invisible Drum Kit — building character monologues that turned a single performer into an entire revue.

name: Sam Kinison
blurb: The scream — rock-and-roll rage as comedy.

name: Sandra Bernhard
blurb: The theatrical solo-show pioneer — stand-up that fused cabaret, rock-and-roll, and Vanity Fair celebrity dissection a decade before anyone called it 'a show.'

name: Shane Gillis
blurb: Throwback club energy for the podcast era.

name: Stavros Halkias
blurb: The self-deprecating id of the podcast scene.

name: Steve Coogan
blurb: The character actor's character actor — an impressionist who workshopped a fictional sports presenter named Alan Partridge on stage until the persona swallowed the man.
photo_credit: Gordon Correll via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 2.0)

name: Steve Martin
blurb: Anti-comedy showman who filled arenas with absurdity.

name: Steven Wright
blurb: The one-liner at absolute zero; pure deadpan.

name: Taylor Tomlinson
blurb: Sharp generational confessional; late-night host.

name: Tig Notaro
blurb: Built a masterpiece set out of catastrophe.
